articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Stricter EIA Norms for Critical Infrastructure Projects
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court today affirmed the validity of recent amendments to the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) Notification, 2006, which introduced more stringent requirements for environmental clearance of critical infrastructure projects.
• The ruling came on a batch of petitions challenging the amendments, arguing they would impede economic development.
• The Court emphasized the 'precautionary principle' and 'sustainable development' as integral to environmental jurisprudence.
• It highlighted the need for robust public consultation and post-clearance monitoring mechanisms, especially for projects in ecologically sensitive zones.
• The judgment is expected to set a new precedent for balancing environmental protection with developmental goals in India.
gsSubTopics: Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)
Environmental protection and conservation
Sustainable development
Government policies and interventions
prelimsPoints: EIA Notification, 2006 and its amendments.
Key principles of environmental jurisprudence: Precautionary Principle, Polluter Pays Principle, Sustainable Development.
Role of Supreme Court in environmental governance.
Different stages of EIA process (screening, scoping, public hearing, appraisal, decision).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of EIA in balancing development and environment.
Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's judgment on future infrastructure projects and industrial development.
Critically evaluate the challenges in implementing EIA norms effectively in India.
Suggest reforms to strengthen environmental governance and public participation in environmental decision-making.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel striking a sound block with a backdrop of green trees and industrial infrastructure, symbolizing environmental law and development.
articles.trivia: India's first major environmental legislation was the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974.

articles.headline: Government Announces New PLI Scheme to Boost Green Hydrogen Manufacturing
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government has unveiled a new Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme specifically targeting the manufacturing of Green Hydrogen and its components.
• The scheme aims to incentivize domestic production, reduce import dependence, and position India as a global hub for Green Hydrogen technology.
• Financial outlays and eligibility criteria for manufacturers of electrolysers and related components have been detailed.
• This initiative is part of India's broader National Green Hydrogen Mission, pushing for decarbonization of key sectors and achieving net-zero targets.
• Experts laud the move as crucial for scaling up the nascent Green Hydrogen industry and attracting significant investments.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Infrastructure: Energy
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: What is Green Hydrogen (produced via electrolysis of water using renewable energy).
Objectives of National Green Hydrogen Mission.
Concept of PLI schemes and their sectors.
Types of hydrogen (grey, blue, green, pink, yellow, turquoise).
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential of Green Hydrogen in India's energy transition and achieving climate goals.
Evaluate the effectiveness of PLI schemes in boosting domestic manufacturing and technological innovation.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for India in becoming a global leader in Green Hydrogen production and export.
Examine the economic and environmental benefits of scaling up Green Hydrogen production.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of a factory producing hydrogen with solar panels and wind turbines in the background, symbolizing green energy.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

articles.headline: India Co-chairs UN Climate Finance Dialogue Ahead of COP30
articles.explainer:
• India today co-chaired a crucial United Nations Climate Finance Dialogue in New York, emphasizing the urgent need for developed nations to meet their $100 billion climate finance commitment.
• The dialogue focused on innovative financing mechanisms, technology transfer, and capacity building for developing countries to tackle climate change.
• India's representative highlighted the disproportionate impact of climate change on vulnerable nations and called for equitable burden-sharing.
• The discussions are critical preparatory steps for the upcoming COP30 summit, aiming to bridge gaps in climate finance pledges and delivery.
• Developing countries reiterated their demand for a new collective quantified goal (NCQG) on climate finance post-2025.
gsSubTopics: Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: What is climate finance and its importance.
Role of UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) and COPs.
The $100 billion climate finance commitment and its status.
Concept of NCQG (New Collective Quantified Goal).
mainsPoints: Analyze India's role and stance in global climate change negotiations, particularly concerning climate finance.
Discuss the challenges in mobilizing and delivering climate finance to developing countries.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current international mechanisms for climate finance and suggest reforms.
Examine the implications of inadequate climate finance for global efforts to combat climate change.
articles.imageDescription: Representatives from various countries seated around a large table with UN logos, engaged in a serious discussion.
articles.trivia: The Paris Agreement (2015) aims to limit global warming to well below 2, preferably to 1.5 degrees Celsius, compared to pre-industrial levels.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Tests Next-Gen Reusable Launch Vehicle Technology
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) today announced the successful completion of a critical test for its next-generation Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) technology.
• The test involved a scaled-down prototype demonstrating autonomous landing capabilities under simulated operational conditions.
• This achievement marks a significant step towards developing cost-effective and sustainable space missions for India, reducing the cost of access to space.
• RLV technology aims to reuse the first stage of rockets, similar to SpaceX's Falcon 9, for multiple launches.
• ISRO plans further tests and a full-scale demonstrator in the coming years, with potential applications for future manned missions and satellite deployments.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: What is RLV technology and its benefits.
ISRO's previous RLV-TD (Technology Demonstrator) missions.
Comparison with global RLV programs (e.g., SpaceX, Blue Origin).
Significance for India's space program and commercial space sector.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of Reusable Launch Vehicle technology for India's space ambitions.
Analyze how RLV technology can revolutionize access to space and foster a vibrant private space sector in India.
Examine the challenges ISRO faces in developing and deploying full-scale RLV systems.
Evaluate the potential economic and scientific impacts of successful RLV implementation for India.
articles.imageDescription: A sleek, futuristic reusable launch vehicle prototype landing vertically on a platform, with ISRO logo visible.
articles.trivia: ISRO's first satellite, Aryabhata, was launched on April 19, 1975, by the Soviet Union.

articles.headline: Parliament Passes 'National Urban Mobility Act' to Streamline City Transport
articles.explainer:
• Both houses of Parliament have passed the landmark 'National Urban Mobility Act, 2025', aimed at providing a comprehensive legal framework for urban transport planning and management.
• The Act seeks to integrate various modes of transport, promote sustainable mobility solutions, and enhance last-mile connectivity in Indian cities.
• Key provisions include the establishment of Urban Metropolitan Transport Authorities (UMTAs) and a National Urban Mobility Fund.
• It also emphasizes data-driven planning, adoption of smart technologies, and non-motorized transport infrastructure.
• This legislation is expected to address chronic issues like traffic congestion, pollution, and inadequate public transport in rapidly urbanizing India.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
prelimsPoints: Key features and objectives of the National Urban Mobility Act, 2025.
Concept of Urban Metropolitan Transport Authorities (UMTAs).
Government initiatives for urban development (e.g., Smart Cities Mission, AMRUT).
Different modes of public transport in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the National Urban Mobility Act in addressing India's urban transport challenges.
Discuss how integrated urban transport planning can contribute to sustainable urban development and improved quality of life.
Evaluate the potential impact of the Act on federal-state relations in urban governance.
Suggest measures for effective implementation of the Act, considering diverse urban contexts in India.
articles.imageDescription: A modern urban landscape showing a metro train, cycle lanes, and electric buses, symbolizing integrated urban mobility.
articles.trivia: Mumbai's local train network carries over 7.5 million commuters daily, one of the busiest in the world.

articles.headline: India and Japan Conduct Joint Maritime Exercise 'JIMEX-25' in Indo-Pacific
articles.explainer:
• The navies of India and Japan commenced the bilateral maritime exercise 'JIMEX-25' in the strategically vital Indo-Pacific region.
• The exercise focuses on enhancing interoperability, maritime security cooperation, and sharing best practices in anti-submarine warfare and maritime surveillance.
• It underscores the growing strategic partnership between India and Japan, particularly in ensuring a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• High-level naval delegations from both countries observed the drills, which included complex tactical maneuvers and joint operations.
• 'JIMEX' series of exercises are crucial for strengthening defense ties and addressing common security challenges in the region.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Name of the exercise (JIMEX) and participating countries (India, Japan).
Location of the exercise (Indo-Pacific region).
Objectives of joint military exercises (interoperability, best practices, regional security).
Other key defense exercises involving India (e.g., Malabar, Varuna, Surya Kiran).
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of India-Japan defense cooperation in the context of Indo-Pacific security.
Discuss how joint maritime exercises like JIMEX contribute to regional stability and counter challenges posed by other powers.
Evaluate the role of bilateral security partnerships in India's broader foreign policy objectives.
Examine the evolving dynamics of maritime security in the Indo-Pacific and India's response.
articles.imageDescription: Two warships, one Indian and one Japanese, sailing in formation on the open sea during a joint exercise.
articles.trivia: The Indian Navy is the seventh-largest in the world by the number of active personnel and is considered a blue-water navy.

articles.headline: New National Biodiversity Action Plan 2025-2030 Launched
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has launched India's 'National Biodiversity Action Plan (NBAP) 2025-2030'.
• This comprehensive plan outlines strategies and targets for biodiversity conservation, sustainable use of biological resources, and equitable sharing of benefits.
• It aligns with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF) and India's commitments under the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD).
• Key focus areas include habitat restoration, invasive species management, climate change adaptation for biodiversity, and mainstreaming biodiversity across economic sectors.
• The plan also emphasizes community participation and traditional knowledge in conservation efforts.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Biodiversity and its conservation.
prelimsPoints: Objectives of the new NBAP 2025-2030.
Connection to international agreements like CBD and GBF.
Key pillars of biodiversity conservation (in-situ, ex-situ).
Role of Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change.
Concept of biodiversity hotspots in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of a national biodiversity action plan in achieving conservation goals in a biodiversity-rich country like India.
Analyze how the new NBAP aligns with global biodiversity targets and India's international commitments.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing biodiversity conservation strategies, particularly concerning human-wildlife conflict and livelihood dependencies.
Suggest policy measures to effectively mainstream biodiversity conservation across various economic sectors.
articles.imageDescription: A vibrant collage of Indian wildlife, flora, and diverse ecosystems, representing rich biodiversity.
articles.trivia: India is one of the 17 megadiverse countries in the world, accounting for 7-8% of the world's recorded species.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Repo Rate, Cautious on Inflation Outlook Amid Global Uncertainties
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) today decided to keep the repo rate unchanged at its latest Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) meeting, citing persistent global uncertainties and a need to assess domestic inflationary pressures.
• The MPC's decision was unanimous, with members emphasizing a 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance to ensure inflation remains within the target band.
• RBI projected a moderate GDP growth for the current fiscal year, acknowledging both domestic consumption resilience and external headwinds.
• Governor Shaktikanta Das reiterated the central bank's commitment to price stability while supporting economic growth.
• Analysts anticipate continued vigilance from RBI, with future rate actions dependent on evolving inflation and growth dynamics.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Monetary policy.
prelimsPoints: Role of RBI and Monetary Policy Committee (MPC).
Definition of Repo Rate, Reverse Repo Rate, CRR, SLR.
RBI's inflation targeting mandate (4% +/- 2%).
Different stances of monetary policy (accommodative, neutral, withdrawal of accommodation, tightening).
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate and its potential implications for the Indian economy.
Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with supporting economic growth.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in addressing supply-side inflation and global economic shocks.
Examine the interplay between fiscal and monetary policy in India's macroeconomic management.
articles.imageDescription: A graph showing a stable horizontal line for interest rates, with Reserve Bank of India building in the background.
articles.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

articles.headline: Ministry of Education Launches 'Digital Classrooms for All' Initiative
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education has inaugurated the 'Digital Classrooms for All' initiative, aiming to equip all government schools with smart classrooms and digital learning resources.
• The program focuses on bridging the digital divide, especially in rural and remote areas, by providing access to high-quality online educational content.
• It includes training for teachers in digital pedagogy and provision of devices like tablets and interactive whiteboards.
• This initiative is a significant step towards realizing the goals of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, promoting equitable and inclusive education.
• Pilot projects have shown promising results in enhancing student engagement and learning outcomes.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry — the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: Objectives of 'Digital Classrooms for All' initiative.
Key recommendations of National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 related to digital education.
Other digital education platforms in India (e.g., SWAYAM, DIKSHA).
Government's efforts to bridge the digital divide.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential of digital education initiatives like 'Digital Classrooms for All' in transforming India's education landscape.
Discuss the challenges in implementing digital learning solutions across diverse socio-economic and geographical contexts in India.
Evaluate how this initiative can contribute to achieving equitable and inclusive education as envisioned in NEP 2020.
Suggest measures to ensure effective utilization of digital infrastructure and resources in schools.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom interacting with an interactive whiteboard and tablets, with a teacher facilitating.
articles.trivia: India has one of the largest school systems in the world, with over 1.5 million schools.

articles.headline: India Pledges Enhanced Support for Global South at G77+China Meeting
articles.explainer:
• India's External Affairs Minister addressed the G77+China Ministerial Meeting, reaffirming India's commitment to strengthening cooperation with the Global South.
• The minister highlighted India's role as a voice for developing countries on issues like climate justice, sustainable development, and equitable global governance.
• India pledged enhanced financial and technical support through various bilateral and multilateral mechanisms to address the development challenges faced by these nations.
• The meeting focused on forging common positions ahead of major UN summits and addressing shared concerns such as debt distress and food security.
• This reiterates India's long-standing policy of South-South Cooperation and its aspiration for a multipolar world order.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: What is the G77+China group and its objectives.
Concept of Global South and South-South Cooperation.
India's historical role in Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) and advocacy for developing countries.
Key issues faced by the Global South (debt, climate change, food security).
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of India's leadership and enhanced support for the Global South in contemporary international relations.
Discuss how South-South Cooperation can address global challenges more effectively than traditional North-South development models.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the G77+China group as a platform for developing countries to articulate their interests.
Examine the implications of a stronger Global South for the future of multilateralism and global governance.
articles.imageDescription: A map highlighting countries of the Global South, with India prominently featured, symbolizing cooperation.
articles.trivia: The Group of 77 (G77) was founded on June 15, 1964, by 77 developing countries at the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD).

articles.headline: High Court Quashes State Government's Decision on Local Body Election Delimitation
articles.explainer:
• The State High Court today quashed a notification issued by the state government regarding the delimitation of wards for upcoming local body elections, citing procedural irregularities.
• The petitioners argued that the delimitation process was arbitrary and politically motivated, violating constitutional provisions for fair representation.
• The Court directed the State Election Commission to conduct a fresh delimitation exercise, ensuring transparency and adherence to established legal norms.
• This ruling highlights the judiciary's role in safeguarding democratic processes at the grassroots level and upholding the principles of electoral fairness.
• The decision is likely to delay the scheduled local body elections in the state.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
prelimsPoints: Role of State Election Commission in local body elections.
Constitutional provisions for local self-government (73rd and 74th Amendment Acts).
Concept of delimitation and its purpose.
Powers of High Courts under Article 226.
mainsPoints: Analyze the importance of fair and transparent delimitation processes for ensuring democratic representation in local self-government.
Discuss the role of the judiciary in upholding electoral integrity and checking arbitrary actions by state governments.
Evaluate the challenges faced by State Election Commissions in conducting free and fair local body elections.
Suggest reforms to strengthen the autonomy and functioning of local self-government institutions in India.
articles.imageDescription: A High Court building with scales of justice in the foreground, symbolizing judicial oversight on democratic processes.
articles.trivia: The 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendment Acts (1992) gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions and Urban Local Bodies, respectively.

articles.headline: India Develops Indigenous AI Model for Advanced Disaster Prediction and Management
articles.explainer:
• Indian scientists and technologists have successfully developed an indigenous Artificial Intelligence (AI) model capable of predicting natural disasters with greater accuracy and lead time.
• The model integrates real-time satellite data, meteorological inputs, seismic readings, and historical patterns to generate early warnings for floods, cyclones, and earthquakes.
• This advanced AI system is expected to significantly enhance India's disaster preparedness and response mechanisms, minimizing loss of life and property.
• It is being integrated into the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) framework for wider deployment.
• The project highlights India's growing capabilities in AI and its application for societal benefit.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Disaster and disaster management.
prelimsPoints: Role of AI in disaster management.
Key functions of NDMA (National Disaster Management Authority).
Different types of natural disasters common in India.
Applications of satellite technology in disaster monitoring.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of Artificial Intelligence in enhancing India's disaster prediction and management capabilities.
Analyze the challenges and ethical considerations associated with deploying AI for critical public safety applications.
Evaluate how an indigenous AI model can strengthen national resilience against natural calamities.
Suggest policy measures to promote research and development in AI for disaster risk reduction in India.
articles.imageDescription: A futuristic control room showing multiple screens displaying weather patterns, satellite imagery, and data visualizations for disaster prediction.
articles.trivia: The Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015-2030) is an international agreement that aims to reduce disaster risk and losses.

articles.headline: Record Agricultural Exports Boost Rural Economy, Government Eyes New Markets
articles.explainer:
• India has achieved record agricultural exports in the last fiscal year, significantly contributing to the rural economy and farmer incomes.
• Key commodities driving this growth include rice, spices, marine products, and fresh fruits and vegetables.
• The government attributes this success to policy support, infrastructure development (e.g., cold chains, logistics), and market access initiatives.
• Efforts are now underway to identify and penetrate new international markets, especially in Southeast Asia and Africa.
• This surge in exports is seen as a crucial step towards doubling farmers' income and strengthening India's position as a global agricultural powerhouse.
gsSubTopics: Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security.
Economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: Major agricultural export commodities from India.
Role of Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority (APEDA).
Government schemes for agricultural infrastructure (e.g., Agri-Infra Fund).
Concept of 'doubling farmers' income'.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to the surge in India's agricultural exports and their impact on the rural economy.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for India in becoming a major global player in agricultural trade.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government policies and infrastructure development in boosting agricultural exports.
Suggest further reforms needed to enhance the competitiveness and sustainability of India's agricultural export sector.
articles.imageDescription: A cargo ship being loaded with containers of agricultural produce at a port, with fields in the background.
articles.trivia: India is the world's largest producer of milk, pulses, and spices.

articles.headline: Western Ghats Region Experiences Unusual Rainfall Patterns, Raising Climate Concerns
articles.explainer:
• Scientists have reported unusual and erratic rainfall patterns across the Western Ghats region, including prolonged dry spells followed by intense, short bursts of rain.
• This deviation from historical monsoon trends is attributed to localized climate change impacts and changes in atmospheric circulation.
• The altered rainfall regime poses significant threats to the region's unique biodiversity, agricultural productivity, and water security.
• Experts warn of increased frequency of flash floods, landslides, and droughts, necessitating urgent adaptation strategies.
• The findings underscore the vulnerability of biodiversity hotspots like the Western Ghats to climate variability.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of world’s physical geography.

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location- changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: Location and significance of Western Ghats as a biodiversity hotspot.
Impact of climate change on monsoon patterns.
Geophysical phenomena associated with erratic rainfall (floods, landslides).
Ecological sensitivity of the Western Ghats (e.g., Gadgil and Kasturirangan reports).
mainsPoints: Analyze the causes and consequences of unusual rainfall patterns in the Western Ghats region, linking them to broader climate change trends.
Discuss the threats posed by altered rainfall regimes to the unique biodiversity and livelihoods dependent on the Western Ghats ecosystem.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current conservation and disaster management strategies in the region.
Suggest integrated approaches for climate change adaptation and resilience building in ecologically sensitive areas like the Western Ghats.
articles.imageDescription: Lush green Western Ghats landscape under heavy rain, with signs of erosion and a river overflowing.
articles.trivia: The Western Ghats is older than the Himalayas and is recognized as one of the world's eight 'hottest hotspots' of biological diversity.

articles.headline: National Commission for Women Proposes Reforms in Cybercrime Laws Against Women
articles.explainer:
• The National Commission for Women (NCW) has submitted a comprehensive set of recommendations to the Ministry of Home Affairs for reforming existing cybercrime laws to better protect women.
• The proposals include stricter penalties for online harassment, cyberstalking, and the non-consensual sharing of intimate images.
• NCW emphasized the need for faster investigation and prosecution mechanisms, along with enhanced digital literacy and awareness campaigns.
• The recommendations also call for greater accountability of social media platforms in curbing online abuse.
• This move comes amidst a rising trend of cybercrimes targeting women and girls across India.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: Mandate and functions of the National Commission for Women (NCW).
Key provisions of the Information Technology Act, 2000, related to cybercrime.
Types of cybercrimes against women.
Government initiatives for women's safety (e.g., Cybercrime Reporting Portal).
mainsPoints: Analyze the increasing threat of cybercrimes against women in India and its socio-psychological impact.
Discuss the gaps and limitations in existing legal frameworks for addressing cybercrime against women.
Evaluate the significance of NCW's recommendations in strengthening the legal and institutional response to online abuse.
Suggest comprehensive strategies, including technological, legal, and educational measures, to enhance women's safety in the digital space.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image of a woman's silhouette against a backdrop of binary code and internet symbols, representing cyber safety.
articles.trivia: The National Commission for Women was set up as a statutory body in January 1992 under the National Commission for Women Act, 1990.

articles.headline: Centre Approves New Framework for Inter-State Water Dispute Resolution
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet has approved a new, streamlined framework for resolving inter-state water disputes, aiming to expedite the process and ensure equitable water sharing.
• The proposed framework includes a permanent tribunal with multiple benches and a mandatory conciliation process before judicial adjudication.
• It emphasizes data sharing, scientific assessment, and a time-bound resolution mechanism to prevent prolonged conflicts.
• The move comes in response to long-pending disputes that have often hampered regional development and cooperation.
• This new approach seeks to replace the existing ad-hoc tribunal system with a more efficient and institutionalized mechanism.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Dispute Redressal Mechanisms and institutions.
prelimsPoints: Constitutional provisions related to inter-state water disputes (Article 262).
Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956.
Key features of the new framework (permanent tribunal, conciliation).
Challenges in inter-state water sharing in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional and legal complexities surrounding inter-state water disputes in India.
Discuss the shortcomings of the existing Inter-State Water Disputes Act and the ad-hoc tribunal system.
Evaluate how the new framework, with its permanent tribunal and conciliation process, can improve water dispute resolution.
Suggest measures to foster cooperation among states for sustainable water resource management in a federal setup.
articles.imageDescription: A map of India showing major rivers and state boundaries, with a symbolic hand holding a water drop over it, representing water sharing.
articles.trivia: Article 262 of the Indian Constitution empowers Parliament to legislate on the adjudication of disputes relating to waters of inter-State rivers.

articles.headline: Global Report Highlights Growing Climate Migration Crisis in South Asia
articles.explainer:
• A new report released by the UN Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR) warns of an escalating climate migration crisis in South Asia, with millions projected to be displaced by 2050.
• The report identifies extreme weather events, rising sea levels, and desertification as primary drivers of internal and cross-border displacement in the region.
• It calls for urgent regional cooperation, enhanced adaptive capacities, and better policy frameworks to manage climate-induced migration.
• India, with its vast coastline and large agricultural population, is particularly vulnerable to these trends.
• The findings underscore the interconnectedness of climate change, human mobility, and regional stability.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
keyTerms: UNDRR (UN Office for Disaster Risk Reduction)
prelimsPoints: Definition of climate migration/climate refugees.
Role of UNDRR (UN Office for Disaster Risk Reduction).
Key climate change impacts leading to migration in South Asia (sea level rise, desertification, extreme weather).
International frameworks for disaster risk reduction (e.g., Sendai Framework).
mainsPoints: Analyze the drivers and consequences of climate migration in South Asia, with a specific focus on India.
Discuss the socio-economic, environmental, and security challenges posed by large-scale climate-induced displacement.
Evaluate the existing national and international policy responses to climate migration and their effectiveness.
Suggest comprehensive strategies for managing climate migration, including adaptation, mitigation, and regional cooperation.
articles.imageDescription: People walking with belongings in a barren, flooded, or drought-affected landscape, symbolizing climate displacement.
articles.trivia: According to the World Bank, South Asia could have 40 million climate migrants by 2050 if no significant climate action is taken.

articles.headline: India Launches Phase 2 of National Quantum Mission, Focus on Commercialization
articles.explainer:
• The Department of Science & Technology (DST) has officially launched Phase 2 of India's National Quantum Mission, shifting focus towards the commercialization and industrial application of quantum technologies.
• Phase 2 will allocate significant funding for developing quantum computing hardware, secure quantum communication networks, and quantum sensing devices.
• It aims to foster deeper collaboration between academia, industry, and startups to translate research breakthroughs into marketable products.
• The mission seeks to position India as a global leader in quantum technologies, essential for future economic and strategic autonomy.
• Training of a specialized workforce and creation of quantum-ready infrastructure are also key objectives.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: Objectives of the National Quantum Mission.
Basic concepts of quantum computing, quantum communication, and quantum sensing.
Key organizations involved in quantum research in India.
Applications of quantum technology in various sectors.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of quantum technology for India's future economic growth and national security.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities in transitioning from basic research to commercialization in quantum technologies.
Evaluate the potential impact of the National Quantum Mission Phase 2 on India's scientific and industrial landscape.
Suggest policy interventions to accelerate the development of a robust quantum ecosystem in India, including talent development and international collaboration.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract image of interconnected nodes and light beams, symbolizing quantum entanglement and communication networks.
articles.trivia: Quantum computers use qubits, which can exist in multiple states simultaneously (superposition), unlike classical bits that are either 0 or 1.

articles.headline: E-Waste Management Rules, 2025 Notified with Extended Producer Responsibility
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has notified the 'E-Waste Management Rules, 2025', replacing previous regulations with a more comprehensive framework.
• The new rules introduce stricter targets for Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR), mandating manufacturers to collect and recycle a higher percentage of their electronic waste.
• They also streamline the authorization process for recyclers and dismantlers, and introduce penalties for non-compliance.
• The aim is to promote a circular economy, reduce environmental pollution from e-waste, and ensure safe disposal and recycling practices.
• Awareness campaigns for consumers on responsible e-waste disposal are also part of the new framework.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
keyTerms: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: Key features of E-Waste Management Rules, 2025.
Concept of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR).
Impact of e-waste on environment and human health.
Importance of a circular economy for waste management.
mainsPoints: Analyze the growing challenge of e-waste management in India and its environmental and health implications.
Discuss how the new E-Waste Management Rules, 2025, aim to promote a circular economy and responsible recycling practices.
Evaluate the effectiveness of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) in ensuring proper e-waste collection and disposal.
Suggest measures to improve consumer awareness and participation in e-waste recycling initiatives.
articles.imageDescription: A pile of discarded electronic gadgets (phones, laptops, wires) being sorted for recycling, with a green leaf symbolizing environmental care.
articles.trivia: India is the third-largest producer of e-waste globally, after China and the United States.

highlights: Supreme Court upholds stricter Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) norms, emphasizing sustainable development.
Union Government launches new PLI scheme to boost Green Hydrogen manufacturing, targeting energy independence.
India co-chairs UN Climate Finance Dialogue, urging developed nations to meet $100 billion commitment ahead of COP30.
ISRO successfully tests next-generation Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) technology, paving way for cost-effective space access.
Parliament passes 'National Urban Mobility Act, 2025' to streamline city transport and promote sustainable solutions.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the recent Supreme Court judgment on Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) norms, consider the following statements:
1. The judgment primarily focuses on increasing economic development by relaxing environmental regulations.
2. The Court emphasized the 'precautionary principle' as integral to environmental jurisprudence.
3. It highlighted the need for robust public consultation in project clearances.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect. The judgment upheld *stricter* EIA norms, emphasizing sustainable development and environmental protection, not relaxing regulations for economic development. Statements 2 and 3 are correct, as the Court explicitly emphasized the 'precautionary principle' and the need for robust public consultation.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements best describes 'Green Hydrogen' as per the recent government initiative?
(a) Hydrogen produced from natural gas with carbon capture and storage technology.
(b) Hydrogen produced through electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources.
(c) Hydrogen produced from coal gasification with emissions offset by afforestation.
(d) Hydrogen produced using nuclear energy for water splitting.
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) Hydrogen produced through electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources.
prelimsMocks.explanation: Green Hydrogen is defined as hydrogen produced by splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen using an electrolyser powered by renewable energy sources like solar or wind. Option (a) describes 'Blue Hydrogen', while (c) and (d) describe other forms of hydrogen production with different environmental footprints.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following about India's National Urban Mobility Act, 2025:
1. It aims to integrate various modes of transport and promote sustainable mobility solutions.
2. It mandates the establishment of Urban Metropolitan Transport Authorities (UMTAs).
3. It primarily focuses on inter-state road connectivity and national highways.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct as the Act aims to integrate modes, promote sustainable mobility, and establish UMTAs. Statement 3 is incorrect; the Act focuses on *urban* transport planning and management within cities, not primarily on inter-state road connectivity or national highways.

prelimsMocks.question: The 'JIMEX-25' exercise, recently conducted in the Indo-Pacific region, involves the navies of which two countries?
prelimsMocks.explanation: JIMEX stands for Japan-India Maritime Exercise. The exercise 'JIMEX-25' involves the navies of India and Japan, focusing on enhancing interoperability and maritime security cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following is NOT a key feature of the newly notified E-Waste Management Rules, 2025?
(a) Stricter targets for Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR).
(b) Streamlined authorization process for recyclers and dismantlers.
(c) Exemption of large manufacturers from e-waste collection targets.
(d) Introduction of penalties for non-compliance with rules.
prelimsMocks.answer: (c) Exemption of large manufacturers from e-waste collection targets.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The new E-Waste Management Rules, 2025, introduce *stricter* targets for Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR), meaning manufacturers, especially larger ones, are mandated to collect and recycle a higher percentage of their electronic waste. Therefore, exempting large manufacturers is contrary to the spirit and provisions of the new rules. Options (a), (b), and (d) are all key features of the rules.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the Supreme Court's decision to uphold stricter Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) norms. Discuss its implications for balancing environmental protection with infrastructure development in India. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment; GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the judgment and its context (EIA amendments). Discuss the 'precautionary principle' and 'sustainable development' as key judicial reasoning. Analyze positive implications (better environmental protection, public participation, long-term sustainability). Discuss potential challenges (project delays, compliance burden, need for efficient clearance). Conclude on the imperative of robust EIA for India's future.

mainsMocks.question: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission, supported by new PLI schemes, aims to position the country as a global hub for Green Hydrogen. Discuss the potential of Green Hydrogen in India's energy transition and examine the challenges in scaling up its production and adoption. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure: Energy; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life; Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation.
mainsMocks.approach: Define Green Hydrogen and the mission's objectives. Discuss its potential: decarbonization, energy security, export opportunities, economic growth. Analyze challenges: high production cost, infrastructure development (storage, transport), technological hurdles (electrolyser efficiency), water availability, policy consistency. Conclude with the need for a multi-pronged strategy.

mainsMocks.question: The newly passed 'National Urban Mobility Act, 2025' seeks to transform urban transport in India. Evaluate the key provisions of this Act and discuss how it can address the multifarious challenges of urban mobility in rapidly growing Indian cities. (10 marks, 150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors; Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
mainsMocks.approach: Briefly introduce the Act and its purpose. List key provisions (integrated planning, UMTAs, sustainable modes, smart tech, NMT infrastructure). Discuss how these provisions tackle challenges like congestion, pollution, last-mile connectivity, and lack of integration. Conclude on the potential for more livable and efficient cities, contingent on effective implementation.

quickBytes.text: The 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendment Acts gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj and Urban Local Bodies, respectively.
India is one of the 17 megadiverse countries, accounting for 7-8% of the world's recorded species.

The Precautionary Principle states that if an action or policy has a suspected risk of causing harm to the public or the environment, in the absence of scientific consensus, the burden of proof that it is not harmful falls on those taking the action.
The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.
The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 aims for universalization of education from pre-school to secondary level with 100% Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) by 2030.
South-South Cooperation refers to the technical cooperation among developing countries in the Global South.
Article 262 of the Indian Constitution grants Parliament the power to legislate on the adjudication of disputes relating to waters of inter-State rivers.
The Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015-2030) is a global agreement aiming to reduce disaster risk and losses.
Quantum computing uses 'qubits' which can exist in multiple states simultaneously, enabling faster computation for specific problems.
India is the world's largest producer of milk, pulses, and spices, and ranks second in fruits and vegetables.

GS1.headline: Western Ghats Region Experiences Unusual Rainfall Patterns, Raising Climate Concerns
GS1.explainer:
• Scientists have reported unusual and erratic rainfall patterns across the Western Ghats region, including prolonged dry spells followed by intense, short bursts of rain.
• This deviation from historical monsoon trends is attributed to localized climate change impacts and changes in atmospheric circulation.
• The altered rainfall regime poses significant threats to the region's unique biodiversity, agricultural productivity, and water security.
• Experts warn of increased frequency of flash floods, landslides, and droughts, necessitating urgent adaptation strategies.
• The findings underscore the vulnerability of biodiversity hotspots like the Western Ghats to climate variability.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of world’s physical geography.

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location- changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: Location and significance of Western Ghats as a biodiversity hotspot.
Impact of climate change on monsoon patterns.
Geophysical phenomena associated with erratic rainfall (floods, landslides).
Ecological sensitivity of the Western Ghats (e.g., Gadgil and Kasturirangan reports).
mainsPoints: Analyze the causes and consequences of unusual rainfall patterns in the Western Ghats region, linking them to broader climate change trends.
Discuss the threats posed by altered rainfall regimes to the unique biodiversity and livelihoods dependent on the Western Ghats ecosystem.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current conservation and disaster management strategies in the region.
Suggest integrated approaches for climate change adaptation and resilience building in ecologically sensitive areas like the Western Ghats.
GS1.imageDescription: Lush green Western Ghats landscape under heavy rain, with signs of erosion and a river overflowing.
GS1.trivia: The Western Ghats is older than the Himalayas and is recognized as one of the world's eight 'hottest hotspots' of biological diversity.

GS2.headline: India Co-chairs UN Climate Finance Dialogue Ahead of COP30
GS2.explainer:
• India today co-chaired a crucial United Nations Climate Finance Dialogue in New York, emphasizing the urgent need for developed nations to meet their $100 billion climate finance commitment.
• The dialogue focused on innovative financing mechanisms, technology transfer, and capacity building for developing countries to tackle climate change.
• India's representative highlighted the disproportionate impact of climate change on vulnerable nations and called for equitable burden-sharing.
• The discussions are critical preparatory steps for the upcoming COP30 summit, aiming to bridge gaps in climate finance pledges and delivery.
• Developing countries reiterated their demand for a new collective quantified goal (NCQG) on climate finance post-2025.
gsSubTopics: Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: What is climate finance and its importance.
Role of UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) and COPs.
The $100 billion climate finance commitment and its status.
Concept of NCQG (New Collective Quantified Goal).
mainsPoints: Analyze India's role and stance in global climate change negotiations, particularly concerning climate finance.
Discuss the challenges in mobilizing and delivering climate finance to developing countries.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current international mechanisms for climate finance and suggest reforms.
Examine the implications of inadequate climate finance for global efforts to combat climate change.
GS2.imageDescription: Representatives from various countries seated around a large table with UN logos, engaged in a serious discussion.
GS2.trivia: The Paris Agreement (2015) aims to limit global warming to well below 2, preferably to 1.5 degrees Celsius, compared to pre-industrial levels.

GS2.headline: Parliament Passes 'National Urban Mobility Act' to Streamline City Transport
GS2.explainer:
• Both houses of Parliament have passed the landmark 'National Urban Mobility Act, 2025', aimed at providing a comprehensive legal framework for urban transport planning and management.
• The Act seeks to integrate various modes of transport, promote sustainable mobility solutions, and enhance last-mile connectivity in Indian cities.
• Key provisions include the establishment of Urban Metropolitan Transport Authorities (UMTAs) and a National Urban Mobility Fund.
• It also emphasizes data-driven planning, adoption of smart technologies, and non-motorized transport infrastructure.
• This legislation is expected to address chronic issues like traffic congestion, pollution, and inadequate public transport in rapidly urbanizing India.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
prelimsPoints: Key features and objectives of the National Urban Mobility Act, 2025.
Concept of Urban Metropolitan Transport Authorities (UMTAs).
Government initiatives for urban development (e.g., Smart Cities Mission, AMRUT).
Different modes of public transport in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the National Urban Mobility Act in addressing India's urban transport challenges.
Discuss how integrated urban transport planning can contribute to sustainable urban development and improved quality of life.
Evaluate the potential impact of the Act on federal-state relations in urban governance.
Suggest measures for effective implementation of the Act, considering diverse urban contexts in India.
GS2.imageDescription: A modern urban landscape showing a metro train, cycle lanes, and electric buses, symbolizing integrated urban mobility.
GS2.trivia: Mumbai's local train network carries over 7.5 million commuters daily, one of the busiest in the world.

GS2.headline: Ministry of Education Launches 'Digital Classrooms for All' Initiative
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education has inaugurated the 'Digital Classrooms for All' initiative, aiming to equip all government schools with smart classrooms and digital learning resources.
• The program focuses on bridging the digital divide, especially in rural and remote areas, by providing access to high-quality online educational content.
• It includes training for teachers in digital pedagogy and provision of devices like tablets and interactive whiteboards.
• This initiative is a significant step towards realizing the goals of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, promoting equitable and inclusive education.
• Pilot projects have shown promising results in enhancing student engagement and learning outcomes.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry — the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: Objectives of 'Digital Classrooms for All' initiative.
Key recommendations of National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 related to digital education.
Other digital education platforms in India (e.g., SWAYAM, DIKSHA).
Government's efforts to bridge the digital divide.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential of digital education initiatives like 'Digital Classrooms for All' in transforming India's education landscape.
Discuss the challenges in implementing digital learning solutions across diverse socio-economic and geographical contexts in India.
Evaluate how this initiative can contribute to achieving equitable and inclusive education as envisioned in NEP 2020.
Suggest measures to ensure effective utilization of digital infrastructure and resources in schools.
GS2.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom interacting with an interactive whiteboard and tablets, with a teacher facilitating.
GS2.trivia: India has one of the largest school systems in the world, with over 1.5 million schools.

GS2.headline: India Pledges Enhanced Support for Global South at G77+China Meeting
GS2.explainer:
• India's External Affairs Minister addressed the G77+China Ministerial Meeting, reaffirming India's commitment to strengthening cooperation with the Global South.
• The minister highlighted India's role as a voice for developing countries on issues like climate justice, sustainable development, and equitable global governance.
• India pledged enhanced financial and technical support through various bilateral and multilateral mechanisms to address the development challenges faced by these nations.
• The meeting focused on forging common positions ahead of major UN summits and addressing shared concerns such as debt distress and food security.
• This reiterates India's long-standing policy of South-South Cooperation and its aspiration for a multipolar world order.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: What is the G77+China group and its objectives.
Concept of Global South and South-South Cooperation.
India's historical role in Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) and advocacy for developing countries.
Key issues faced by the Global South (debt, climate change, food security).
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of India's leadership and enhanced support for the Global South in contemporary international relations.
Discuss how South-South Cooperation can address global challenges more effectively than traditional North-South development models.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the G77+China group as a platform for developing countries to articulate their interests.
Examine the implications of a stronger Global South for the future of multilateralism and global governance.
GS2.imageDescription: A map highlighting countries of the Global South, with India prominently featured, symbolizing cooperation.
GS2.trivia: The Group of 77 (G77) was founded on June 15, 1964, by 77 developing countries at the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD).

GS2.headline: High Court Quashes State Government's Decision on Local Body Election Delimitation
GS2.explainer:
• The State High Court today quashed a notification issued by the state government regarding the delimitation of wards for upcoming local body elections, citing procedural irregularities.
• The petitioners argued that the delimitation process was arbitrary and politically motivated, violating constitutional provisions for fair representation.
• The Court directed the State Election Commission to conduct a fresh delimitation exercise, ensuring transparency and adherence to established legal norms.
• This ruling highlights the judiciary's role in safeguarding democratic processes at the grassroots level and upholding the principles of electoral fairness.
• The decision is likely to delay the scheduled local body elections in the state.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
prelimsPoints: Role of State Election Commission in local body elections.
Constitutional provisions for local self-government (73rd and 74th Amendment Acts).
Concept of delimitation and its purpose.
Powers of High Courts under Article 226.
mainsPoints: Analyze the importance of fair and transparent delimitation processes for ensuring democratic representation in local self-government.
Discuss the role of the judiciary in upholding electoral integrity and checking arbitrary actions by state governments.
Evaluate the challenges faced by State Election Commissions in conducting free and fair local body elections.
Suggest reforms to strengthen the autonomy and functioning of local self-government institutions in India.
GS2.imageDescription: A High Court building with scales of justice in the foreground, symbolizing judicial oversight on democratic processes.
GS2.trivia: The 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendment Acts (1992) gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions and Urban Local Bodies, respectively.

GS2.headline: National Commission for Women Proposes Reforms in Cybercrime Laws Against Women
GS2.explainer:
• The National Commission for Women (NCW) has submitted a comprehensive set of recommendations to the Ministry of Home Affairs for reforming existing cybercrime laws to better protect women.
• The proposals include stricter penalties for online harassment, cyberstalking, and the non-consensual sharing of intimate images.
• NCW emphasized the need for faster investigation and prosecution mechanisms, along with enhanced digital literacy and awareness campaigns.
• The recommendations also call for greater accountability of social media platforms in curbing online abuse.
• This move comes amidst a rising trend of cybercrimes targeting women and girls across India.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: Mandate and functions of the National Commission for Women (NCW).
Key provisions of the Information Technology Act, 2000, related to cybercrime.
Types of cybercrimes against women.
Government initiatives for women's safety (e.g., Cybercrime Reporting Portal).
mainsPoints: Analyze the increasing threat of cybercrimes against women in India and its socio-psychological impact.
Discuss the gaps and limitations in existing legal frameworks for addressing cybercrime against women.
Evaluate the significance of NCW's recommendations in strengthening the legal and institutional response to online abuse.
Suggest comprehensive strategies, including technological, legal, and educational measures, to enhance women's safety in the digital space.
GS2.imageDescription: A stylized image of a woman's silhouette against a backdrop of binary code and internet symbols, representing cyber safety.
GS2.trivia: The National Commission for Women was set up as a statutory body in January 1992 under the National Commission for Women Act, 1990.

GS2.headline: Centre Approves New Framework for Inter-State Water Dispute Resolution
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet has approved a new, streamlined framework for resolving inter-state water disputes, aiming to expedite the process and ensure equitable water sharing.
• The proposed framework includes a permanent tribunal with multiple benches and a mandatory conciliation process before judicial adjudication.
• It emphasizes data sharing, scientific assessment, and a time-bound resolution mechanism to prevent prolonged conflicts.
• The move comes in response to long-pending disputes that have often hampered regional development and cooperation.
• This new approach seeks to replace the existing ad-hoc tribunal system with a more efficient and institutionalized mechanism.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Dispute Redressal Mechanisms and institutions.
prelimsPoints: Constitutional provisions related to inter-state water disputes (Article 262).
Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956.
Key features of the new framework (permanent tribunal, conciliation).
Challenges in inter-state water sharing in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional and legal complexities surrounding inter-state water disputes in India.
Discuss the shortcomings of the existing Inter-State Water Disputes Act and the ad-hoc tribunal system.
Evaluate how the new framework, with its permanent tribunal and conciliation process, can improve water dispute resolution.
Suggest measures to foster cooperation among states for sustainable water resource management in a federal setup.
GS2.imageDescription: A map of India showing major rivers and state boundaries, with a symbolic hand holding a water drop over it, representing water sharing.
GS2.trivia: Article 262 of the Indian Constitution empowers Parliament to legislate on the adjudication of disputes relating to waters of inter-State rivers.

GS3.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Stricter EIA Norms for Critical Infrastructure Projects
GS3.explainer:
• The Supreme Court today affirmed the validity of recent amendments to the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) Notification, 2006, which introduced more stringent requirements for environmental clearance of critical infrastructure projects.
• The ruling came on a batch of petitions challenging the amendments, arguing they would impede economic development.
• The Court emphasized the 'precautionary principle' and 'sustainable development' as integral to environmental jurisprudence.
• It highlighted the need for robust public consultation and post-clearance monitoring mechanisms, especially for projects in ecologically sensitive zones.
• The judgment is expected to set a new precedent for balancing environmental protection with developmental goals in India.
gsSubTopics: Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)
Environmental protection and conservation
Sustainable development
Government policies and interventions
prelimsPoints: EIA Notification, 2006 and its amendments.
Key principles of environmental jurisprudence: Precautionary Principle, Polluter Pays Principle, Sustainable Development.
Role of Supreme Court in environmental governance.
Different stages of EIA process (screening, scoping, public hearing, appraisal, decision).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of EIA in balancing development and environment.
Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's judgment on future infrastructure projects and industrial development.
Critically evaluate the challenges in implementing EIA norms effectively in India.
Suggest reforms to strengthen environmental governance and public participation in environmental decision-making.
GS3.imageDescription: A gavel striking a sound block with a backdrop of green trees and industrial infrastructure, symbolizing environmental law and development.
GS3.trivia: India's first major environmental legislation was the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974.

GS3.headline: Government Announces New PLI Scheme to Boost Green Hydrogen Manufacturing
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Government has unveiled a new Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme specifically targeting the manufacturing of Green Hydrogen and its components.
• The scheme aims to incentivize domestic production, reduce import dependence, and position India as a global hub for Green Hydrogen technology.
• Financial outlays and eligibility criteria for manufacturers of electrolysers and related components have been detailed.
• This initiative is part of India's broader National Green Hydrogen Mission, pushing for decarbonization of key sectors and achieving net-zero targets.
• Experts laud the move as crucial for scaling up the nascent Green Hydrogen industry and attracting significant investments.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Infrastructure: Energy
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: What is Green Hydrogen (produced via electrolysis of water using renewable energy).
Objectives of National Green Hydrogen Mission.
Concept of PLI schemes and their sectors.
Types of hydrogen (grey, blue, green, pink, yellow, turquoise).
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential of Green Hydrogen in India's energy transition and achieving climate goals.
Evaluate the effectiveness of PLI schemes in boosting domestic manufacturing and technological innovation.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for India in becoming a global leader in Green Hydrogen production and export.
Examine the economic and environmental benefits of scaling up Green Hydrogen production.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration of a factory producing hydrogen with solar panels and wind turbines in the background, symbolizing green energy.
GS3.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Tests Next-Gen Reusable Launch Vehicle Technology
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) today announced the successful completion of a critical test for its next-generation Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) technology.
• The test involved a scaled-down prototype demonstrating autonomous landing capabilities under simulated operational conditions.
• This achievement marks a significant step towards developing cost-effective and sustainable space missions for India, reducing the cost of access to space.
• RLV technology aims to reuse the first stage of rockets, similar to SpaceX's Falcon 9, for multiple launches.
• ISRO plans further tests and a full-scale demonstrator in the coming years, with potential applications for future manned missions and satellite deployments.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: What is RLV technology and its benefits.
ISRO's previous RLV-TD (Technology Demonstrator) missions.
Comparison with global RLV programs (e.g., SpaceX, Blue Origin).
Significance for India's space program and commercial space sector.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of Reusable Launch Vehicle technology for India's space ambitions.
Analyze how RLV technology can revolutionize access to space and foster a vibrant private space sector in India.
Examine the challenges ISRO faces in developing and deploying full-scale RLV systems.
Evaluate the potential economic and scientific impacts of successful RLV implementation for India.
GS3.imageDescription: A sleek, futuristic reusable launch vehicle prototype landing vertically on a platform, with ISRO logo visible.
GS3.trivia: ISRO's first satellite, Aryabhata, was launched on April 19, 1975, by the Soviet Union.

GS3.headline: India and Japan Conduct Joint Maritime Exercise 'JIMEX-25' in Indo-Pacific
GS3.explainer:
• The navies of India and Japan commenced the bilateral maritime exercise 'JIMEX-25' in the strategically vital Indo-Pacific region.
• The exercise focuses on enhancing interoperability, maritime security cooperation, and sharing best practices in anti-submarine warfare and maritime surveillance.
• It underscores the growing strategic partnership between India and Japan, particularly in ensuring a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• High-level naval delegations from both countries observed the drills, which included complex tactical maneuvers and joint operations.
• 'JIMEX' series of exercises are crucial for strengthening defense ties and addressing common security challenges in the region.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Name of the exercise (JIMEX) and participating countries (India, Japan).
Location of the exercise (Indo-Pacific region).
Objectives of joint military exercises (interoperability, best practices, regional security).
Other key defense exercises involving India (e.g., Malabar, Varuna, Surya Kiran).
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of India-Japan defense cooperation in the context of Indo-Pacific security.
Discuss how joint maritime exercises like JIMEX contribute to regional stability and counter challenges posed by other powers.
Evaluate the role of bilateral security partnerships in India's broader foreign policy objectives.
Examine the evolving dynamics of maritime security in the Indo-Pacific and India's response.
GS3.imageDescription: Two warships, one Indian and one Japanese, sailing in formation on the open sea during a joint exercise.
GS3.trivia: The Indian Navy is the seventh-largest in the world by the number of active personnel and is considered a blue-water navy.

GS3.headline: New National Biodiversity Action Plan 2025-2030 Launched
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has launched India's 'National Biodiversity Action Plan (NBAP) 2025-2030'.
• This comprehensive plan outlines strategies and targets for biodiversity conservation, sustainable use of biological resources, and equitable sharing of benefits.
• It aligns with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF) and India's commitments under the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD).
• Key focus areas include habitat restoration, invasive species management, climate change adaptation for biodiversity, and mainstreaming biodiversity across economic sectors.
• The plan also emphasizes community participation and traditional knowledge in conservation efforts.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Biodiversity and its conservation.
prelimsPoints: Objectives of the new NBAP 2025-2030.
Connection to international agreements like CBD and GBF.
Key pillars of biodiversity conservation (in-situ, ex-situ).
Role of Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change.
Concept of biodiversity hotspots in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of a national biodiversity action plan in achieving conservation goals in a biodiversity-rich country like India.
Analyze how the new NBAP aligns with global biodiversity targets and India's international commitments.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing biodiversity conservation strategies, particularly concerning human-wildlife conflict and livelihood dependencies.
Suggest policy measures to effectively mainstream biodiversity conservation across various economic sectors.
GS3.imageDescription: A vibrant collage of Indian wildlife, flora, and diverse ecosystems, representing rich biodiversity.
GS3.trivia: India is one of the 17 megadiverse countries in the world, accounting for 7-8% of the world's recorded species.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Repo Rate, Cautious on Inflation Outlook Amid Global Uncertainties
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) today decided to keep the repo rate unchanged at its latest Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) meeting, citing persistent global uncertainties and a need to assess domestic inflationary pressures.
• The MPC's decision was unanimous, with members emphasizing a 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance to ensure inflation remains within the target band.
• RBI projected a moderate GDP growth for the current fiscal year, acknowledging both domestic consumption resilience and external headwinds.
• Governor Shaktikanta Das reiterated the central bank's commitment to price stability while supporting economic growth.
• Analysts anticipate continued vigilance from RBI, with future rate actions dependent on evolving inflation and growth dynamics.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Monetary policy.
prelimsPoints: Role of RBI and Monetary Policy Committee (MPC).
Definition of Repo Rate, Reverse Repo Rate, CRR, SLR.
RBI's inflation targeting mandate (4% +/- 2%).
Different stances of monetary policy (accommodative, neutral, withdrawal of accommodation, tightening).
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate and its potential implications for the Indian economy.
Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with supporting economic growth.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in addressing supply-side inflation and global economic shocks.
Examine the interplay between fiscal and monetary policy in India's macroeconomic management.
GS3.imageDescription: A graph showing a stable horizontal line for interest rates, with Reserve Bank of India building in the background.
GS3.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

GS3.headline: India Develops Indigenous AI Model for Advanced Disaster Prediction and Management
GS3.explainer:
• Indian scientists and technologists have successfully developed an indigenous Artificial Intelligence (AI) model capable of predicting natural disasters with greater accuracy and lead time.
• The model integrates real-time satellite data, meteorological inputs, seismic readings, and historical patterns to generate early warnings for floods, cyclones, and earthquakes.
• This advanced AI system is expected to significantly enhance India's disaster preparedness and response mechanisms, minimizing loss of life and property.
• It is being integrated into the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) framework for wider deployment.
• The project highlights India's growing capabilities in AI and its application for societal benefit.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Disaster and disaster management.
prelimsPoints: Role of AI in disaster management.
Key functions of NDMA (National Disaster Management Authority).
Different types of natural disasters common in India.
